figures.gloss: A diligent farmer of Ōtani village, Bitchū (1814–1883), whose string of family deaths and his own near-fatal illness at the unlucky age of forty-two were read as the wrath of the dreaded Konjin — until the deity revealed itself as a loving parent. From 1859 until his death he sat daily in his home's mediation hall, relaying petitions to the kami and the kami's replies to petitioners, and was recognized as Ikigami Konko Daijin, a living kami.
figures.facet: Sainthood as sitting still: his charisma was not ecstasy or miracle-working but twenty-four years of unbroken daily availability — divinity demonstrated as perfect attendance at a desk between god and neighbor.
figures.provenance: Konko Daijin Oboegaki (the founder's memoir, written 1874–1881); Gorikai (recorded teachings, Konkōkyō Kyōten); Delwin B. Schneider, Konkokyo: A Japanese Religion (1962).

figures.gloss: The 'Golden Kami of Heaven and Earth' — the parent kami of the universe, whose body is heaven and earth themselves and within whom all people live. This is the feared calendrical demon Konjin, scourge of unlucky directions, unmasked as a benevolent parent who was never wrathful at all: only misunderstood.
figures.facet: The rehabilitation of a folk terror into universal parenthood — Konkokyo's founding theological move, made a generation before Oomoto repeated it, dissolving directional taboo entirely: no day or direction is unlucky within the parent's body.

figures.gloss: The tradition's central practice: a mediator sits at the hiromae (worship hall) and relays between kami and petitioner — hearing a person's trouble, conveying it to Tenchi Kane no Kami, and speaking back the kami's response. Begun by the founder in 1859, the seat has been occupied by his successors in an unbroken line, kept open every day at the Konko headquarters.
figures.facet: Revelation bureaucratized in the gentlest sense — not a onetime prophet's book but a permanently staffed relay window between realms, where the mediation itself, not doctrine, is the religion's living center.

figures.gloss: The brief creedal text the founder wrote out for followers, displayed in every Konkokyo church: 'Ikigami Konko Daijin, Tenchi Kane no Kami — pray with a single heart. The divine favor depends upon one's own heart, on this very day, pray.' A whole soteriology in a few lines.
figures.facet: A canon compressed to a placard: where its Bakumatsu peers produced volumes of revealed scripture, Konkokyo's charter text fits on a wall — locating salvation in the disposition of the heart today rather than in a revealed cosmology.

figures.gloss: The teaching that kami and humanity are fulfilled through each other: the kami is realized through the salvation of people, and people flourish through the kami — neither complete alone. The founder's oracles state that the deity, too, is helped when humans are helped.
figures.facet: A rare theological symmetry: divinity that openly needs humanity, framing worship not as submission to the omnipotent but as two parties completing one another — reciprocity raised to first principle.

edges.note: Konkokyo grew inside the Shinto world and was sanctioned in 1900 as one of the thirteen Kyōha Shintō sects — its universal parent kami emerged from the folk-Shinto Konjin cult while its liturgy and priesthood remained Shinto in form.

Bakumatsu-era peasant founders of the same wave — Nakayama Miki (from 1838) and Kawate Bunjirō (from 1859), each seized by a kami revealed as the parent of humanity and each transformed into a living channel; parallel careers, no documented contact.
Two 19th-century Japanese reconceptions of kami as the single parent deity of the universe, intimate rather than judging — the parent-god pattern arising twice in the same generation.

Documented lineage: Deguchi Nao encountered Konjin faith through Konkokyo, and her early group operated under Konkokyo affiliation (c. 1893–1897) before Oomoto's independence — the fountainhead religion's own fountainhead.

The same folk deity rehabilitated twice in one lineage: the feared Konjin becomes Konkokyo's parent of the universe, and via Nao's Konkokyo-mediated encounter becomes Oomoto's banished creator — attested descent, differently theologized.

Near-simultaneous founder revelations in East Asia (1859 and 1860): a rural man's encounter with the supreme deity revealed as intimately present, launching a mediating new religion amid late-dynastic crisis — parallel emergence, no contact claimed.
Intra-tradition: mediation is mutual fulfillment enacted — each relay across the hiromae desk completes both parties, kami and petitioner, exactly as the principle claims.

dossier.essence: A Japanese new religion founded in 1859 in Ōtani village, Okayama, when the farmer Kawate Bunjirō (1814-1883) — recognized by followers as Ikigami Konko Daijin, a living kami — accepted the deity's request to give up farming and devote himself wholly to mediating between kami and humanity. It teaches that the feared calendrical demon Konjin is in truth Tenchi Kane no Kami, the benevolent parent of the universe; sanctioned in 1900 as one of the thirteen sects of Kyōha Shintō, it remains a mid-sized denomination headquartered at Konkō-chō, with churches across Japan and in the overseas diaspora.
dossier.cosmos: Heaven and earth are the body of Tenchi Kane no Kami, within whom all people live — so no day or direction is unlucky, for no place lies outside the parent. Kami and humanity fulfill one another (aiyo kakeyo): the deity is realized through the salvation of people and people flourish through the kami, neither complete alone. Divine favor turns on the disposition of one's own heart 'on this very day,' and death is understood as a return into the care of the kami within whose body one has always lived.
dossier.practice: The center of practice is toritsugi, mediation: a mediator sits daily at the hiromae worship hall, hears each petitioner's trouble, conveys it to Tenchi Kane no Kami, and speaks back the kami's response — a seat kept open at the Konkō headquarters in unbroken succession since 1859. Worship is Shinto in form, with daily services and festivals, but the tradition imposes no directional taboos, austerities, or divination; what it asks is sincerity of heart (magokoro), gratitude, and prayer 'with a single heart,' as the Tenchi Kakitsuke placard displayed in every church directs.
dossier.canon: The Konko Daijin Oboegaki (the founder's memoir, written 1874-1881) and the Konkōkyō Kyōten, which gathers the recorded teachings (Gorikai) and the brief creedal Tenchi Kakitsuke.
dossier.reflects: Revelation as permanent institution — a staffed, daily relay window between the human and the divine in place of a closed prophetic canon.
sources: Delwin B. Schneider, 'Konkokyo: A Japanese Religion' (1962)
Helen Hardacre, 'Kurozumikyo and the New Religions of Japan' (1986)

links.cite: Delwin B. Schneider, 'Konkokyo: A Japanese Religion' (1962)
Helen Hardacre, 'Kurozumikyo and the New Religions of Japan' (1986)
